prompt: Which work in the corpus contains a passage in which the divine instructor — speaking in the first person to his pupil — confesses his own back-story: he names himself the seventh of an eight-fold succession of teaching deities (Ananta, Sūkṣma, Śivatama, Ekarudra, Ekanetra, Trimūrti, Śrīkaṇṭha, Śikhaṇḍī) in which each successor replaces the previous, identifies himself as 'that Śrīkaṇṭha,' and admits that having been struck by passion he gazed upon the god of love so that the body of Kāmadeva 'was shattered, incinerated, and turned to ash' — yet 'the passion remained in me,' which is why he took Lady Umā as his wife and fathered the holy Sanatkumāra? Quote the succession-list and the Kāmadeva-and-Umā confession verbatim, and identify the work and chapter.
rationale: The Aṣṭavidyāsana succession sequence (Ananta → … → Śrīkaṇṭha → Śikhaṇḍī), the first-person identification 'I am that Śrīkaṇṭha,' the poison-sight incineration of Kāmadeva, and the explicit naming of Sanatkumāra as the resulting son are unique to Vrhaspatitattva among the corpus's Old Javanese Saiva texts (Tattvajñāna, Mahājñāna, Gaṇapatitattva contain none of these); no Sanskrit smṛti, dharmaśāstra digest, Iqbal/Ghalib/Zauq divan, Cynewulf poem, or Comte/Dunoyer treatise contains this autobiographical Śrīkaṇṭha-Kāma frame.

prompt: Which work in the corpus likens the cosmic veil of illusion to a honeycomb crowded with souls — declaring that 'māyā is like the honeycomb, and the ātmā is like the young bees,' that they are 'face-down, which means they are bent over,' and that each soul 'looks only downward, unaware of the tattva above it'? The same passage explains that this honey-comb soul-state arises because Śiva-tattva, originally crystal-clear, becomes stained by mala (impurity) and so 'ceases to be all-knowing and all-doing,' at which point it is renamed ātmā, meaning 'dazed consciousness.' Quote the honeycomb-and-young-bees image verbatim and identify the work and chapter.
rationale: The honeycomb-and-young-bees simile for the multitude of souls in māyātattva, paired with the 'face-down / bent over / looks only downward' image and the explicit gloss of ātmā as 'dazed consciousness,' is unique to Vrhaspatitattva; the kindred Old Javanese Saiva texts (Tattvajñāna, Mahājñāna, Gaṇapatitattva) describe māyā and crystal-clear Śiva-tattva but use neither the honeycomb image nor the 'young bees looking only downward' phrasing, and no Sanskrit smṛti or non-Indic corpus work contains this specific bee-simile for souls under impurity.

prompt: Which work in the corpus stages an elephant-and-blind-men parable in a teacher-pupil dialogue, in which the questioner asks why his teacher has bestowed so many different scriptures (naming the three paths Śaiva, Pāśupata, and Alepaka), and is answered with a passage that compares confused seekers to a group of blind men feeling different parts of an elephant — one calling it a water jar, one a winnowing fan, one a snake, one a granary, one an eel, one a pillar — and then coins the technical term andhatattva ('the principle of the blind men') to name the resulting state of bewilderment? Quote the six blind-men-comparisons verbatim and identify the work and chapter.
rationale: The pairing of the blind-men-and-elephant parable with these specific six Old-Javanese-Saiva analogies (head = water jar, ear = winnowing fan, trunk = snake, belly = granary, tail = eel, leg = pillar), the explicit framing it within the question 'Śaiva or Pāśupata or Alepaka?', and the coinage of the technical term andhatattva are all unique to Vrhaspatitattva — none of Tattvajñāna, Mahājñāna, Gaṇapatitattva, the Sanskrit smṛtis, or any other corpus work uses this andhatattva coinage or this six-part Śaiva-Pāśupata-Alepaka frame around the parable.
